title: Brown soda bread
subtitle: Wholemeal Irish soda bread, ready in under an hour
excerpt: A dense, nutty wholemeal loaf leavened only with bicarbonate of soda and buttermilk. No yeast, no kneading, no resting. It mixes in five minutes and comes out of the oven in forty.
sourceNotes: Brown soda bread is the everyday loaf of the Irish kitchen, made before domestic yeast was reliable and when wholemeal flour was the staple grain. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), documents the soda-bread method; the Irish form is documented in pre-1928 Irish household guides and agricultural extension material. The acid-alkali reaction (buttermilk and bicarbonate of soda) as a leavening method predates commercial yeast in the British Isles.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze the slices flat. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Soda bread is best eaten the day it is made; after 24 hours it becomes dense. Mix and bake to order.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cutting a deep cross into the top of a soda bread before baking serves two purposes: it allows the loaf to expand in the oven without cracking at the sides, and it divides the baked loaf into four sections that break apart cleanly. The cuts should go almost to the base of the loaf, not just score the surface.

content.text: Soda bread is a practical loaf, not a showpiece. The leavening is chemical: bicarbonate of soda reacts with the acid in buttermilk and produces carbon dioxide as the loaf bakes. This means the dough must go straight into the oven once mixed; any sitting around and the gas escapes before the crumb sets. Mix quickly, shape once, bake immediately.

Preheat the oven to 200°C conventional and line a baking tray with baking paper. Mix the flours, bicarbonate of soda, and salt in a large bowl. Pour in the buttermilk and stir with a wooden spoon or a fork until just combined into a rough, shaggy dough. Do not overwork it. Turn out onto a lightly floured surface and shape quickly into a round about 18 cm across; the dough is too soft to knead, so work it with light hands.
Lift onto the prepared tray. Using a sharp knife, cut

deep into the surface, taking the cuts almost to the base of the loaf. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the crust is firm and dark, and the base sounds hollow when you tap it. An internal probe should read at least 95°C. Transfer to a wire rack and leave to cool for 20 minutes before cutting; cutting too soon makes the crumb gummy.

If you have no buttermilk, mix 350 ml of whole milk with 1 tablespoon of lemon juice or white wine vinegar and leave for 5 minutes. It is not identical to buttermilk but produces a comparable reaction.
